1	# Architecture Notes
2	
3	## After cycle 1
4	- Change: extracted module-level `const PROCESSING_FEE = 5` and used it in the return literal instead of a bare `5`.
5	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) ‚Äî spec calls this "processing fee (R3)"; naming the literal makes the domain concept explicit and prepares for upcoming quote tests that all add +5 fee. Mass rose 2 ‚Üí 3 but clarity trumps APP.
6	- Naming established: `PROCESSING_FEE` (module-private constant) for the fixed R3 fee component of a quote premium.
7	
8	## After cycle 2
9	- Change: renamed local `base` to `basePremium` inside `runScenario`'s step.map callback so it mirrors the module-level `BASE_PREMIUM` lookup table and reads explicitly in the premium formula.
10	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) ‚Äî `base` alone is ambiguous (base of what?); `basePremium` matches the domain term used in the spec ("100 base") and ties directly to the `BASE_PREMIUM` map it is summed from. Mass unchanged at 30. Did not extract a `quoteStep` helper yet ‚Äî too early with only 2 tests; will reconsider once item-modifier tests (curse, high-enchantment) land.
11	- Naming established: `basePremium` (local) for the sum of `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type]` across items in one quote step.
12	
13	## After cycle 3
14	- Change: extracted explaining variable `firstInsurance = basePremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE` so the premium formula reads as a sum of three named domain components: `basePremium + firstInsurance + PROCESSING_FEE`.
15	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) ‚Äî the spec describes "first insurance" as a distinct premium component (10% of base), parallel to `PROCESSING_FEE`; the formula now mirrors that vocabulary directly. Mass +1 (one binding) but Rule 2 > APP per project rules. This sets up cleanly for upcoming additive components (curse surcharge, high-enchantment surcharge, loyalty discount) ‚Äî each will become its own named line in the same shape.
16	- Naming established: `firstInsurance` (local) for the 10% first-insurance premium component on `basePremium`.
17	
18	## After cycle 4
19	- No refactoring (justified): Green added one row (`staff: 80`) to the existing `BASE_PREMIUM` table ‚Äî purely additive data, no structural change. All names still reveal intent (`BASE_PREMIUM`, `basePremium`, `firstInsurance`, `PROCESSING_FEE` match spec vocabulary), no duplication present, formula reads as three named domain components. Extracting a `quoteStep`/`computeBasePremium` helper is still premature: per cycle-2 plan, defer until item-modifier tests (curse, high-enchantment) land ‚Äî the next todo (`potion`) is yet another base-premium row, not a modifier. Touching the `?? 0` fallback now would pre-empt the dedicated CLI-error tests for unknown items further down the list.
20	
21	## After cycle 5
22	- No refactoring (justified): Green added one row (`potion: 40`) to `BASE_PREMIUM` ‚Äî same purely-additive shape as cycle 4. No new structural opportunities emerged. Names still match spec vocabulary; no duplication; the three-component formula (basePremium + firstInsurance + PROCESSING_FEE) still reads as the spec describes. Inlining the `results` binding would save 1 mass unit but hurt Rule 2 (the named `results` mirrors the output key). Extracting `computeBasePremium(items)` is still premature ‚Äî the natural trigger is the 3-rune block-discount test (cycle 8 in the todo list), where pure lookup-and-sum first breaks down; refactoring now would be guessing at the helper's shape before seeing how component blocks fit. The `?? 0` fallback continues to be reserved for the CLI unknown-item-type error test later.
23	
24	## After cycle 6
25	- Change: extracted explaining variable `rawPremium = basePremium + firstInsurance + PROCESSING_FEE` so the line `const premium = Math.ceil(rawPremium)` reads as a discrete rounding step.
26	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) ‚Äî the rune test (32.5 ‚Üí 33) made rounding-up a distinct domain step (spec R3: "rounded up to the nearest G"). Naming the unrounded total separates "compose the premium from its named components" from "round up to whole G"; both intents now stand out at a glance. Mass +1 (one binding) but Rule 2 > APP. Did not extract a `quoteStep`/`computeBasePremium` helper yet ‚Äî per cycles 2/4/5, that's reserved for the 3-rune block-discount test where pure lookup-and-sum breaks down; the next test (2 runes, no block) is still pure summation.
27	- Naming established: `rawPremium` (local) for the unrounded sum of premium components, prior to round-up.
28	
29	## After cycle 7
30	- No refactoring (justified): Green made no code change ‚Äî the 2-runes test auto-passed via the existing reduce+lookup (25+25=50 base, √ó1.1=55, +5=60). Six candidates were considered and rejected: (1) extract `computeQuotePremium(items)` helper ‚Äî natural trigger is the 3-rune block-discount test (cycle 8 per the todo list) where pure lookup-and-sum breaks down; extracting now is still guessing at the helper's shape, same reasoning as cycles 2/4/5/6; (2) inline `rawPremium` ‚Äî would reverse cycle 6's Rule 2 win; (3) inline `results` ‚Äî already rejected in cycle 5 on Rule 2 grounds (mirrors output-key vocabulary); (4) inline the `scenario` cast ‚Äî named cast on its own line is the conventional TS narrowing pattern; (5) tighten `runScenario(input: unknown)` signature ‚Äî reserved for the CLI-error tests further down the list; (6) extract the reducer lambda ‚Äî single call site, two short tokens, and the `?? 0` is tied to the deferred unknown-item-type CLI-error test. All names still match spec vocabulary; mass remains around 41 with no Rule-2-respecting reduction available.
31	
32	## After cycle 8
33	- Change: named the two magic numbers in the rune block-discount branch ‚Äî introduced module-level `const RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE = 3` and `const RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE = 60`, and used them in the `if (type === "rune" && count === RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE) basePremium += RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE` branch.
34	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) ‚Äî Green introduced bare `3` and `60` for spec R2 (rune block discount); naming them ties the branch directly to spec vocabulary ("block of 3 runes" ‚Üí "block price"). Mass +4 (two bindings + two constants at module scope) but Rule 2 > APP per project rules. Deferred again: extracting a `computeBasePremium(items)` / `priceForType(type, count)` helper or a table-driven block-discount rule ‚Äî the next two tests ("4 runes no block", "3 runes + 3 moonstones") will reshape the conditional from `count === 3` into "count of complete 3-blocks across alike-typed components"; refactoring now would guess at that shape. Also deferred: renaming `perItem` (short name, 6-line scope ‚Äî clear enough), tightening `runScenario(input: unknown)` (reserved for CLI-error tests).
35	- Naming established: `RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE` (module-private constant) for the spec-R2 block size of 3 runes; `RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE` (module-private constant) for the price of one complete rune block.
36	
37	## After cycle 9
38	- Change: moved `const perItem = BASE_PREMIUM[type] ?? 0` from the top of the for-loop into the `else` branch where it is actually used; the block branch (`count === RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE`) no longer computes a value it ignores.
39	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) ‚Äî `perItem` now sits next to its only consumer (`basePremium += perItem * count`), making the else-branch read as a self-contained per-item pricing step; the if-branch reads as a self-contained block-priced step. Rule 4 (Fewest Elements) ‚Äî the block branch no longer carries a dead binding/lookup. APP static element count is unchanged (~64) since the elements still exist in the else-branch, but the dead path in the block branch is gone. Did NOT extract `computeBasePremium(items)` / `priceForType(type, count)` helper or generalize the block rule to `Math.floor(count / RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE)` arithmetic ‚Äî both are still premature per cycles 2/4/5/6/7/8: the natural trigger is "3 runes + 3 moonstones" (cycle 11 in the todo list), where the conditional MUST generalize from "type === rune" to "any block-eligible type" and from `count === 3` to "complete 3-blocks of one type". Refactoring now would guess at that shape; the 7-rune test (cycle 10) still falls through the else-branch cleanly (7*25=175). Locality move is reversible if the block rule restructures the branch entirely.
40	- Naming established: none new (`perItem` retained in narrower scope).
41	
42	## After cycle 10
43	- No refactoring (justified): Green made no code change ‚Äî the 7-runes test auto-passed via the existing else-branch summation (7 √ó 25 = 175 base, √ó1.1 = 192.5, +5 = 197.5 ‚Üí ceil 198). The test exercised the same `BASE_PREMIUM[type] * count` path that the 4-runes test already covered (cycle 9), so no new domain concept or structural pressure emerged. Six candidates considered and rejected: (1) extract `computeBasePremium(items)` / `priceForType(type, count)` helper ‚Äî natural trigger is "3 runes + 3 moonstones" (next test in the todo list), where the conditional MUST generalize from `type === "rune"` to "any block-eligible type"; extracting now would guess at the helper's shape, same reasoning as cycles 2/4/5/6/7/8/9; (2) generalize block rule to `Math.floor(count / RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE) * RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE + (count % RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE) * perItem` arithmetic ‚Äî the 7-rune test actually CONFIRMS the rule is "exactly one full block of 3 ‚Üí block price; else per-item" (7 runes priced at 7√ó25, not 60+4√ó25=160), so block-arithmetic would over-fit and break the 4-rune and 7-rune tests; (3) rename `perItem` to `perItemPrice` ‚Äî lives in a 2-line scope, multiplied by `count` immediately, rename adds mass with no Rule-2 win; (4) inline `rawPremium` / `firstInsurance` / `basePremium` ‚Äî reverses cycles 3/6 Rule-2 wins; (5) tighten `runScenario(input: unknown)` signature ‚Äî reserved for CLI-error tests further down the list; (6) extract the count-by-type loop into `countByType(items)` ‚Äî single call site, 3 lines, well-named local; adds invocation mass without Rule-2 win, defer until a second call site appears (claim-handling tests may bring one). All names still match spec vocabulary; mass remains around 65 with no Rule-2-respecting reduction available.
44

content.text:
All 11 tests pass. Now let me evaluate the refactor opportunities.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation

- `runScenario` ‚Äî describes what it does (executes a scenario). Still fits.
- `BASE_PREMIUM` ‚Äî table of per-item base premiums. Fits spec vocabulary.
- `basePremium`, `firstInsurance`, `rawPremium`, `PROCESSING_FEE` ‚Äî all match spec terms.
- `countsByType` ‚Äî accurately describes the per-type count map (just added moonstone row).
- `perItem` ‚Äî clear in narrow scope (cycles 9 retained this naming).
- `RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE` / `RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE` ‚Äî spec-aligned.

The recent change (cycle 11, moonstone row) was purely additive data ‚Äî adding `moonstone: 25` to the `BASE_PREMIUM` table. The test "2 runes + 1 moonstone" passed via the else-branch summation (2√ó25 + 1√ó25 = 75, √ó1.1 = 82.5, +5 = 87.5 ‚Üí ceil 88). No naming change indicated.

## Step 2: Calculate Current APP Mass

```
const PROCESSING_FEE = 5;                                        // bind+const = 2
const FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE = 0.1;                                // bind+const = 2
const BASE_PREMIUM: Record<string, number> = { 6 entries };      // 1 bind + 6 keys + 6 consts = 13
const RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE = 3;                                       // 2
const RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE = 60;                                     // 2

export function runScenario(input: unknown): unknown {           // 1 bind (input)
  const scenario = input as Scenario;                            // 1 bind + 1 ref = 2
  const results = scenario.steps.map((step) => {                 // 1 bind + 1 inv (map) + 1 loop-callback + 1 bind (step) = ~8 (map counts as loop=5 + bind results=1 + bind step=1 = 7)
    const countsByType: Record<string, number> = {};             // 1 bind + 1 const ({}) = 2
    for (const item of step.items) {                             // 1 loop + 1 bind = 6
      countsByType[item.type] = (countsByType[item.type] ?? 0) + 1;  // 1 assign + 2 refs + 1 const + 2 invocations (??, +) = ~10
    }
    let basePremium = 0;                                         // 1 bind + 1 const = 2
    for (const [type, count] of Object.entries(countsByType)) {  // 1 loop + 2 bind + 1 inv = 8
      if (type === "rune" && count === RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE) {        // 1 cond + 1 const + 2 invocations = ~7
        basePremium += RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE;                         // 1 assign = 6
      } else {
        const perItem = BASE_PREMIUM[type] ?? 0;                 // 1 bind + 1 inv + 1 const = 4
        basePremium += perItem * count;                          // 1 assign + 1 inv = 8
      }
    }
    const firstInsurance = basePremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE;   // 1 bind + 1 inv = 3
    const rawPremium = basePremium + firstInsurance + PROCESSING_FEE;  // 1 bind + 2 inv = 5
    const premium = Math.ceil(rawPremium);                       // 1 bind + 1 inv = 3
    return { premium };                                          // 1 const = 1
  });
  return { results };                                            // 1 const = 1
}
```

Approximate total mass: ~85 (stable since cycle 10).

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

### Rule 1: Tests Pass ‚úÖ
All 11 tests passing.

### Rule 2: Reveals Intent
The new test "2 runes + 1 moonstone ‚Üí base 75 G (no block: different types)" makes the **"alike components only"** rule concrete in the spec. The current implementation's block branch is gated on `type === "rune"` ‚Äî which already enforces "only runes form blocks". But the spec's R2 talks about **blocks of alike components** broadly. The current code's literal `"rune"` is fine since runes are the only block-eligible type so far. The condition reads exactly as the spec's currently-tested constraint.

### Rule 3: No Duplication
No duplication of consequence.

### Rule 4: Fewest Elements
No unused elements.

## Step 4: Evaluate Candidate Refactorings

Let me enumerate candidates:

1. **Extract `computeBasePremium(items)` / `priceForType(type, count)` helper** ‚Äî this is the long-deferred refactoring (cycles 2/4/5/6/7/8/9/10). With cycle 11 ("2 runes + 1 moonstone"), the conditional now demonstrably handles mixed-type counts via per-type iteration. **However, the next test ("3 runes + 3 moonstones ‚Üí base 120 G; two separate blocks") will FORCE generalization of the block rule from `type === "rune"` to "any block-eligible type" (or to all types). Extracting now would still guess at that shape.** Same reasoning as prior cycles.

2. **Generalize block rule to "any type with count === RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE"** ‚Äî would over-fit. Currently moonstone has no block discount per the as-yet-unimplemented "3 moonstones" test. The next test (`3 runes + 3 moonstones`) is what determines whether moonstones get the same block. Premature.

3. **Inline `countsByType` build into a reduce** ‚Äî single use, the for-loop reads explicitly. Reduce form would add an invocation and an arrow callback for no Rule-2 win.

4. **Rename `perItem` ‚Üí `perItemPrice`** ‚Äî lives in 2-line scope, multiplied by `count` on the next line. Adds 1 binding mass, no clarity win.

5. **Extract per-step quote callback into named function `quoteStep(step)`** ‚Äî single call site (the `.map`), but it WOULD give the per-step pipeline a name. However, with the next test ("3 runes + 3 moonstones") almost certainly reshaping the type-counting loop (or block-eligibility logic), extracting now would freeze a shape that's about to change.

6. **Tighten `input: unknown` ‚Üí typed signature** ‚Äî reserved for CLI-error tests.

7. **Rename `BASE_PREMIUM` to reflect that "moonstone: 25" is identical to "rune: 25"** ‚Äî no, the spec lists each item type independently; the table is a flat lookup, not a derived structure.

8. **Replace `if (type === "rune" && count === RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE)` with a `BLOCK_PRICES: Record<string, number>` table** ‚Äî would express "rune has a block discount" as data, parallel to `BASE_PREMIUM`. But cycle 11 doesn't yet constrain whether other types have block discounts. Premature.

## Step 5: Decision ‚Äî No Refactoring This Cycle

Every candidate either:
- Guesses at a shape the next test ("3 runes + 3 moonstones") will fix, or
- Reverses a prior cycle's Rule-2 win, or
- Adds mass with no clarity gain.

The mixed-type test (cycle 11) was specifically the test that revealed the block branch IS correctly gated by `type === "rune"` (moonstones fell through the else-branch and produced the right answer). Confirming the conditional's current shape is correct; reshaping it now would be guessing.

## Step 6: Document Decision

```
Refactoring Evaluation (cycle 11):
- Naming: All names still match spec vocabulary (BASE_PREMIUM, basePremium,
  firstInsurance, rawPremium, PROCESSING_FEE, RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE, RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE,
  countsByType, perItem).
- Duplication: None of consequence.
- Simplification: No dead code; cycle 9 already moved perItem into its
  consumer branch.
- Mass: ~85, no Rule-2-respecting reduction available.

The "2 runes + 1 moonstone" test was the first mixed-type test and
confirmed that gating the block on `type === "rune"` (not `count === 3`
alone) correctly excluded the moonstone path. The very next test
("3 runes + 3 moonstones ‚Üí two separate blocks") will determine whether
the block rule generalizes from "rune" to "any block-eligible type" ‚Äî
extracting a helper or table-driven block rule now would guess at that
shape.

No refactoring performed.
```

## Step 7: Tests Status

## After cycle 10
- No refactoring (justified): Green made no code change ‚Äî the 7-runes test auto-passed via the existing else-branch summation (7 √ó 25 = 175 base, √ó1.1 = 192.5, +5 = 197.5 ‚Üí ceil 198). The test exercised the same `BASE_PREMIUM[type] * count` path that the 4-runes test already covered (cycle 9), so no new domain concept or structural pressure emerged. Six candidates considered and rejected: (1) extract `computeBasePremium(items)` / `priceForType(type, count)` helper ‚Äî natural trigger is "3 runes + 3 moonstones" (next test in the todo list), where the conditional MUST generalize from `type === "rune"` to "any block-eligible type"; extracting now would guess at the helper's shape, same reasoning as cycles 2/4/5/6/7/8/9; (2) generalize block rule to `Math.floor(count / RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE) * RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE + (count % RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE) * perItem` arithmetic ‚Äî the 7-rune test actually CONFIRMS the rule is "exactly one full block of 3 ‚Üí block price; else per-item" (7 runes priced at 7√ó25, not 60+4√ó25=160), so block-arithmetic would over-fit and break the 4-rune and 7-rune tests; (3) rename `perItem` to `perItemPrice` ‚Äî lives in a 2-line scope, multiplied by `count` immediately, rename adds mass with no Rule-2 win; (4) inline `rawPremium` / `firstInsurance` / `basePremium` ‚Äî reverses cycles 3/6 Rule-2 wins; (5) tighten `runScenario(input: unknown)` signature ‚Äî reserved for CLI-error tests further down the list; (6) extract the count-by-type loop into `countByType(items)` ‚Äî single call site, 3 lines, well-named local; adds invocation mass without Rule-2 win, defer until a second call site appears (claim-handling tests may bring one). All names still match spec vocabulary; mass remains around 65 with no Rule-2-respecting reduction available.

## After cycle 11
- No refactoring (justified): Green added one row (`moonstone: 25`) to `BASE_PREMIUM` ‚Äî purely additive data, same shape as cycles 4/5. The mixed-type test ("2 runes + 1 moonstone ‚Üí base 75 G, no block") was the first to exercise the per-type iteration with two distinct types, and it CONFIRMED the existing block branch is correctly gated on `type === "rune"` (the moonstone fell through the else-branch and produced 25, never colliding with the block path). Eight candidates considered and rejected: (1) extract `computeBasePremium(items)` / `priceForType(type, count)` helper ‚Äî natural trigger is now the very next test, "3 runes + 3 moonstones ‚Üí two separate blocks", which will force the block branch to generalize from `type === "rune"` to "any block-eligible type" (or all types); extracting now would guess at that shape, same reasoning as cycles 2/4/5/6/7/8/9/10; (2) replace `if (type === "rune" && count === RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE)` with a `BLOCK_PRICES: Record<string, number>` table ‚Äî premature; cycle 11 doesn't yet constrain whether moonstones or other types have block discounts, the next test will; (3) generalize block to "any type at count === RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE" ‚Äî would over-fit before "3 runes + 3 moonstones" determines whether moonstones share the rule; (4) inline `countsByType` into a reduce ‚Äî single use, the for-loop reads explicitly, reduce form adds invocation + arrow mass with no Rule-2 win; (5) extract the per-step `.map` callback into named `quoteStep(step)` ‚Äî single call site, and the next test will likely reshape the type-counting/block logic inside it; freezing a shape now is premature; (6) rename `perItem` to `perItemPrice` ‚Äî 2-line scope, multiplied by `count` on the very next line, rename adds 1 binding with no clarity win (re-rejected from cycle 10); (7) tighten `runScenario(input: unknown)` signature ‚Äî reserved for CLI-error tests; (8) inline `rawPremium` / `firstInsurance` / `basePremium` ‚Äî reverses cycles 3/6 Rule-2 wins. All names still match spec vocabulary; mass remains around 85 (one extra row in BASE_PREMIUM, +2 elements vs cycle 10) with no Rule-2-respecting reduction available.

content.text:
The current single-cycle refactor task (one decision, justified, one notes-file append) is not multi-step enough to warrant TodoWrite tracking, so I'll skip it.

---

Refactor Phase Complete (cycle 11):

**Refactoring**: None applied ‚Äî justified.

**Reasoning**: The recent Green added one row (`moonstone: 25`) to `BASE_PREMIUM` ‚Äî purely additive data. The "2 runes + 1 moonstone" test was the first mixed-type exercise of the per-type iteration; it confirmed the block branch is correctly gated on `type === "rune"` (the moonstone fell through the else-branch cleanly). Every candidate refactoring (extract helper, table-driven block, generalize block, inline counts, extract per-step callback, rename `perItem`, tighten signature, inline named components) either guesses at the shape the next test ("3 runes + 3 moonstones ‚Üí two separate blocks") will force, or reverses a prior cycle's Rule-2 win, or adds mass without clarity gain.

**Mass**: ~85, stable (+2 vs cycle 10 for the new table row).

**Tests**: 11 passed, 24 todo, all green.

Relevant paths:
- /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-20_23-17-45_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7/src/claim-office.ts
- /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-20_23-17-45_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7/src/claim-office.spec.ts
- /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-20_23-17-45_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7/architecture-notes.md

Proceeding to the next test.
